does: This paper introduces Qwen-Scope, a comprehensive suite of sparse autoencoders (SAEs) trained on 14 groups across 7 variants of the Qwen model family, including both dense and mixture-of-expert architectures. Beyond using SAEs for post-hoc interpretability analysis, the work demonstrates four practical development applications: inference-time steering that controls model outputs by manipulating SAE feature directions without weight modification, evaluation analysis using activated features as representation-level proxies for understanding benchmark performance, and additional steering and analysis capabilities. The paper positions SAEs as active development tools rather than passive analysis instruments, providing concrete methods for intervening on model behavior and understanding model decisions through sparse feature decompositions.
connects: This work builds on the mechanistic interpretability research program that seeks to understand neural network decision-making by decomposing activations into interpretable components. It directly extends the sparse autoencoder framework, which has emerged as a leading approach for discovering interpretable features in large language models, moving beyond earlier probing and attention analysis methods. The paper responds to limitations in prior SAE work by scaling these tools across multiple model variants and architectures, and by demonstrating that SAEs can serve purposes beyond post-hoc analysis. It connects to prior work on representation steering, model editing, and activation intervention techniques, synthesizing these directions into a unified suite that combines interpretation with practical control mechanisms.
enables: This work opens pathways for using interpretability tools as first-class components in model development workflows. The public release of Qwen-Scope enables researchers to systematically steer model behavior and audit decision-making without gradient-based model editing, potentially reducing risks from unintended side effects. It establishes foundations for building interpretability-aware training procedures where sparse features inform optimization objectives, and for developing better evaluation metrics based on feature activation patterns. The work suggests future directions in layer-wise steering, fine-grained preference control, and using interpretable feature decompositions to guide model alignment, debugging, and capability evaluation across different scales and architectures.
